ground_truth_requirements.text: Database The heart of an IOC is a memory resident database together with various memory resident structures describing the contents of the database. EPICS supports a large and extensible set of record types, e.g. ai (Analog Input), ao (Analog Output), etc. Each record type has a ﬁxed set of ﬁelds. Some ﬁelds are common to all record types and others are speciﬁc to particular record types. Every record has a record name and every ﬁeld has a ﬁeld name. The ﬁrst ﬁeld of every database record holds the record name, which must be unique across all IOCs attached to the same TCP/IP subnet. A number of data structures are provided so that the database can be accessed efﬁciently. Most software components, because they access the database via database access routines, do not need to be aware of these structures. Databases Access With the exception of record and device support, all access to the database is via the channel or database access routines. Databases Scanning Database scanning is the mechanism for deciding when to process a record. Four types of scanning are possible: Periodic, Event, I/O Event, and Passive. Periodic: A request can be made to process a record periodically. A number of time intervals are supported. Event: Event scanning is based on the posting of an event by any IOC software component. The actual subroutine call is: post_event(event_num) I/O Event: The I/O event scanning system processes records based on external interrupts. An IOC device driver interrupt routine must be available to accept the external interrupts. Passive: Passive records are processed as a result of linked records being processed or as a result of external changes such as channel access puts. Record Support, Device Support, and Device Drivers In order to remove record speciﬁc knowledge from database access, each record type has an associated record support module. Similarly, in order to remove device speciﬁc knowledge from record support, each record type can have a set of device support modules. If the method of accessing hardware is complicated, a device driver can be provided to shield the device support modules. Many record types, in particular all types not associated with hardware, do not have device support or drivers. The IOC software is designed so that the database access layer knows nothing about the record support layer other than how to call it. The record support layer in turn knows nothing about it’s device support layer other than how to call it. Similarly the only thing a device support layer knows about it’s associated driver is how to call it. This design allows a particular installation and even a particular IOC within an installation to choose the set of record types, device types, and drivers it wishes to use. The remainder of the IOC system software is unaffected. Every record support module must provide a record processing routine. It is this routine that is called by the database scanners. Record processing consists of some combination of a standard set of functions. Databases Monitors The routines described in this section provide a callback mechanism for database value changes. This allows the caller to be notiﬁed when database values change without constantly polling the database. A mask can be set to specify value changes, alarm state changes, and/or archive changes. At the present time only channel access uses database monitors. No other software should use the database monitors. Because they are of interest only to channel access, the monitor routines will not be described.
ground_truth_requirements.label: FR

ground_truth_requirements.text: Another large group of predominately Applications developers that are starting work are the Collaborative Access Teams (CATS) at Argonne’s Advanced Photon Source (APS).The APS is a facility to produce high brilliance X-rays for a variety of experiments and includes 36 experimental halls with 2 lines each. As of March 1994, there were 15 CATS at the APS with most CATS responsible for a single sector (2 beamlines). Each CAT has its own funding for designing/building/operating the physical beamline, instrumentation, controls, and data acquisition systems. Because the experimental areas must be coordinated with the main facility control system and since 25% of the beamtime must be made available to independent investigators it was strongly recommended that EPICS be used as the toolkit for all CATS development work. Not only would this simplify the integration with the central control system but would also provide a common environment for facility visitors. Typically, each CAT has 1 or 2 developers, and the major focus is not on the IOC software although some new records, like the Multichannel Analyser, have been created, but rather on the OPI CA client applications. Heavy use is being made of both Tcl/TK and IDL to support high-level sequencing and data reduction. This is similar to the Gemini architecture as the bulk of the control system intelligence is contained within OPI CA client tasks. Other items of concern are: Another large group of predominately Applications developers that are starting work are the Collaborative Access Teams (CATS) at Argonne’s Advanced Photon Source (APS).The APS is a facility to produce high brilliance X-rays for a variety of experiments and includes 36 experimental halls with 2 lines each. generated_requirements.text: The system shall continuously read temperature data from thermostats installed in each of the twelve zones across the three-floor office building.
generated_requirements.rationale: Continuous temperature monitoring is the core sensing function of THEMAS.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support exactly twelve thermostat zones distributed across three floors of the office building.
generated_requirements.rationale: The physical building layout defines the scope of zones the system must manage.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall compare each zone's current temperature reading against its configured temperature setting to determine whether heating or cooling action is required.
generated_requirements.rationale: Temperature comparison against settings is the primary decision-making mechanism for unit control.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall compare each zone's current temperature reading against configurable trigger thresholds to determine when to activate or deactivate heating and cooling units.
generated_requirements.rationale: Trigger thresholds provide hysteresis control to prevent rapid cycling of HVAC units.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall automatically activate a heating or cooling unit for a zone when the zone's temperature crosses the configured trigger threshold.
generated_requirements.rationale: Automatic unit activation is the primary control output of the thermostat management system.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall automatically deactivate a heating or cooling unit for a zone when the zone's temperature returns within the acceptable range relative to its configured setting.
generated_requirements.rationale: Automatic deactivation prevents energy waste once a zone reaches its target temperature.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce a configurable maximum limit on the number of heating and cooling units that may operate simultaneously across the building.
generated_requirements.rationale: Simultaneous unit limits protect electrical infrastructure from overload.

generated_requirements.text: When a unit activation request cannot be fulfilled because the simultaneous unit limit has been reached, the system shall place the request in a queue for deferred processing.
generated_requirements.rationale: Queuing overflow requests ensures no zone's heating or cooling need is permanently ignored.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall process queued unit activation requests in order as running units are deactivated and capacity becomes available.
generated_requirements.rationale: Orderly queue processing ensures fair and predictable unit scheduling.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall trigger a distinct audible alarm on the supervisor's workstation when a temperature reading falls outside the defined valid range for a zone.
generated_requirements.rationale: Out-of-range alarms alert the supervisor to potentially faulty sensors or extreme conditions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall trigger a distinct audible alarm on the supervisor's workstation when a temperature reading exceeds the defined deviation limit from the zone's configured setting.
generated_requirements.rationale: Deviation alarms notify the supervisor of zones that are failing to reach their target temperature.

generated_requirements.text: The audible alarms for out-of-range readings and deviation-limit exceedances shall be acoustically distinct from one another so that the supervisor can identify the alarm type without viewing the screen.
generated_requirements.rationale: Distinct alarm sounds enable rapid situational awareness without requiring visual attention.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a dedicated supervisor interface through which the building supervisor can view the current temperature and status of all twelve zones.
generated_requirements.rationale: A consolidated status view allows the supervisor to monitor the entire building at a glance.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the building supervisor to adjust the configured temperature setting for any individual zone through the supervisor interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: Adjustable settings give the supervisor control over comfort levels in each zone.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the building supervisor to manually reset an active alarm through the supervisor interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: Manual alarm reset allows the supervisor to acknowledge and clear alarms after investigation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the building supervisor to manually turn a heating or cooling unit on or off for any zone through the supervisor interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: Manual override capability gives the supervisor direct control for maintenance or emergency situations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall log every heating or cooling unit status change event (activation and deactivation) to the Microsoft Access database, including the zone identifier, unit type, timestamp, and trigger source.
generated_requirements.rationale: Unit status change logs provide a complete operational audit trail for the building.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall log every denied unit activation request to the Microsoft Access database, including the zone identifier, unit type, timestamp, and reason for denial.
generated_requirements.rationale: Logging denied requests enables analysis of capacity constraint impacts on building comfort.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall log every alarm occurrence and alarm reset event to the Microsoft Access database, including the zone identifier, alarm type, timestamp, and supervisor action taken.
generated_requirements.rationale: Alarm logs support post-incident review and regulatory compliance.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the building supervisor to generate an operational history report from the Microsoft Access database covering a user-specified date range.
generated_requirements.rationale: Operational history reports enable supervisors to review past system behavior and incidents.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the building supervisor to generate a monthly statistical summary report from the Microsoft Access database, including aggregate counts of unit activations, alarms, and denied requests per zone.
generated_requirements.rationale: Monthly summaries support facilities management decision-making and trend analysis.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the configurable simultaneous unit limit to be modified by the supervisor through the supervisor interface without requiring a system restart.
generated_requirements.rationale: Runtime configurability of the unit limit allows adaptation to changing electrical capacity conditions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow the valid temperature range boundaries and deviation limits for each zone to be configured independently through the supervisor interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: Per-zone configuration supports different comfort or operational requirements across building areas.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall poll all twelve thermostat sensors and update zone temperature readings at a configurable interval not exceeding 60 seconds.
generated_requirements.rationale: A maximum 60-second polling interval ensures timely detection of temperature changes requiring action.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall activate or deactivate a heating or cooling unit within 5 seconds of determining that a trigger threshold has been crossed.
generated_requirements.rationale: Fast unit response time minimizes temperature deviation and improves occupant comfort.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall sound the audible alarm on the supervisor's workstation within 3 seconds of detecting an out-of-range or deviation-limit exceedance condition.
generated_requirements.rationale: Rapid alarm notification reduces the time to supervisor awareness of critical conditions.

generated_requirements.text: The supervisor interface shall load and display the current status of all twelve zones within 2 seconds of being opened or refreshed.
generated_requirements.rationale: Fast interface response ensures the supervisor can quickly assess building status.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall write each log entry to the Microsoft Access database within 2 seconds of the triggering event.
generated_requirements.rationale: Timely database writes ensure the event log accurately reflects real-time system activity.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be available for operation at least 99.5% of the time during building operational hours, excluding scheduled maintenance windows.
generated_requirements.rationale: High availability is critical to ensure continuous climate control across the building.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall recover from an unplanned software failure and resume normal monitoring and control operations within 60 seconds without manual intervention.
generated_requirements.rationale: Automatic recovery minimizes the period during which building climate control is unmanaged.

generated_requirements.text: In the event of a database connection failure, the system shall buffer log entries locally and persist them to the Microsoft Access database once connectivity is restored.
generated_requirements.rationale: Local buffering prevents loss of event log data during transient database outages.

generated_requirements.text: If communication with a thermostat sensor is lost, the system shall raise an alarm, suspend automated control decisions for the affected zone, and log the communication failure.
generated_requirements.rationale: Graceful degradation on sensor failure prevents incorrect control actions based on stale data.

generated_requirements.text: Access to the supervisor interface shall require authentication with a unique username and password before any configuration or control actions are permitted.
generated_requirements.rationale: Authentication prevents unauthorized personnel from altering system settings or overriding controls.

generated_requirements.text: All supervisor actions performed through the interface (configuration changes, manual overrides, alarm resets) shall be recorded in the audit log with the authenticated user's identity and timestamp.
generated_requirements.rationale: User-attributed audit logging supports accountability and forensic investigation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce role-based access control, ensuring that only authorized supervisor accounts can modify temperature settings, unit limits, and alarm thresholds.
generated_requirements.rationale: Role-based access prevents accidental or malicious misconfiguration by unauthorized users.

generated_requirements.text: The supervisor interface shall follow a consistent layout and labeling convention, clearly identifying each zone by floor number and zone identifier on all screens.
generated_requirements.rationale: Consistent, clearly labeled layouts reduce supervisor errors and training time.

generated_requirements.text: The supervisor interface shall display active alarms with a visual indicator (e.g., color highlight and alarm type label) in addition to the audible alert, so the supervisor can identify the affected zone and alarm type at a glance.
generated_requirements.rationale: Combined visual and audible alerts improve alarm discoverability in noisy environments.

generated_requirements.text: The supervisor interface shall provide confirmation prompts before executing irreversible or high-impact actions such as manually shutting down a heating or cooling unit.
generated_requirements.rationale: Confirmation prompts reduce the risk of accidental high-impact actions by the supervisor.

generated_requirements.text: The system's source code shall be organized into clearly separated modules for sensor reading, control logic, alarm management, user interface, and data logging, each with documented interfaces.
generated_requirements.rationale: Modular architecture reduces the effort required to isolate and fix defects or add features.

generated_requirements.text: All configurable parameters (polling interval, unit limit, temperature thresholds, deviation limits) shall be stored in a centralized configuration file or database table, not hard-coded in the application source.
generated_requirements.rationale: Externalized configuration allows parameter changes without recompiling or redeploying the system.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall include inline code documentation and a technical maintenance manual sufficient for a qualified developer to understand, modify, and extend the system without access to the original developers.
generated_requirements.rationale: Comprehensive documentation reduces long-term maintenance costs and knowledge dependency.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be designed so that adding support for additional thermostat zones requires changes only to configuration data and not to core application logic.
generated_requirements.rationale: Configuration-driven zone management simplifies future building expansions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall run on the Microsoft Windows operating system and shall not require any third-party runtime components beyond Microsoft Access and the .NET Framework.
generated_requirements.rationale: Limiting runtime dependencies reduces deployment complexity in a standard Windows office environment.

generated_requirements.text: The system's data storage layer shall use Microsoft Access as the database backend, and the database schema shall be documented to facilitate future migration to an alternative DBMS if required.
generated_requirements.rationale: Documented schema supports potential future migration without loss of historical data.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall prevent any single zone's manual override from causing the total number of simultaneously running units to exceed the configured maximum limit, unless the supervisor explicitly acknowledges an override warning.
generated_requirements.rationale: Enforcing the unit limit even during manual overrides protects electrical infrastructure integrity.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall display the current number of active heating and cooling units and the configured maximum simultaneous unit limit on the supervisor interface at all times.
generated_requirements.rationale: Real-time capacity visibility helps the supervisor make informed manual control decisions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall retain all event log records in the Microsoft Access database for a minimum of 24 months before they are eligible for archival or deletion.
generated_requirements.rationale: Long-term log retention supports historical trend analysis and compliance requirements.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall generate and display monthly statistical summary reports within 10 seconds of the supervisor initiating the report request.
generated_requirements.rationale: Acceptable report generation time ensures the reporting feature is practical for routine supervisor use.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall validate all temperature setting inputs entered by the supervisor, rejecting values outside the physically plausible range and displaying a descriptive error message.
generated_requirements.rationale: Input validation prevents misconfiguration that could cause erroneous control decisions or alarms.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain a persistent queue state so that, following a system restart, any pending unit activation requests that were queued before the restart are restored and processed in their original order.
generated_requirements.rationale: Persistent queue state ensures no zone's deferred heating or cooling request is silently lost during restarts.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide consumers with a web-based interface to browse the registry of XML tags, schemas, and related documents.
generated_requirements.rationale: Consumers must be able to discover available XML resources through an accessible interface.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide consumers with full-text and metadata search capabilities to locate XML tags and schemas within the registry.
generated_requirements.rationale: Search functionality is essential for consumers to efficiently find relevant XML resources across a large registry.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow consumers to validate XML documents against registered schemas through the web-based registry interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: Validation ensures that consumer-produced XML documents conform to established defense data standards.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall allow consumers to download XML tags, schemas, and related documents from the registry.
generated_requirements.rationale: Consumers must be able to retrieve and reuse standardized XML resources in their own applications.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide managers with the ability to define, enforce, and modify XML naming conventions for all registered tags and schemas.
generated_requirements.rationale: Consistent naming conventions are critical for semantic interoperability across the defense information enterprise.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide managers with the ability to create, modify, suspend, and delete user accounts and assign roles to producers, consumers, and other managers.
generated_requirements.rationale: User permission governance is required to control access and maintain registry integrity.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide managers with tools to monitor and maintain the integrity of the XML registry, including detection and resolution of duplicate or conflicting entries.
generated_requirements.rationale: Registry integrity is essential to ensure reliable and consistent data exchange across defense organizations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement Discretionary Access Control (DAC) to restrict read and write access to XML registry resources based on user identity and group membership.
generated_requirements.rationale: DAC is required to protect sensitive registry resources from unauthorized access.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall implement Mandatory Access Control (MAC) to enforce security labels and clearance levels on XML registry resources in accordance with defense security policies.
generated_requirements.rationale: MAC is required to enforce multilevel security classifications mandated by military information security policy.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall integrate with COTS Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) products for persistent storage of XML metadata, schemas, and registry entries.
generated_requirements.rationale: Integration with existing defense database infrastructure avoids redundant investment and leverages proven storage solutions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall expose standard APIs (e.g., ODBC, JDBC, or equivalent) to enable interoperability with heterogeneous platforms and third-party defense applications.
generated_requirements.rationale: Standard APIs are necessary to support data management across the diverse heterogeneous platforms used in defense environments.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support data replication across distributed nodes to maintain registry consistency between fixed installations and forward-deployed units.
generated_requirements.rationale: Replication ensures data consistency and availability across geographically dispersed military operational environments.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support distributed transaction management to ensure atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability (ACID) of registry operations across multiple nodes.
generated_requirements.rationale: Transaction management prevents data corruption and ensures reliable registry updates in a distributed environment.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall operate in a COE (Common Operating Environment)-compliant manner, conforming to applicable DII COE standards and specifications.
generated_requirements.rationale: COE compliance is mandated for all defense information infrastructure components to ensure interoperability.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support multiple operational modes including peacetime training, maintenance, crisis, and wartime operations without requiring reconfiguration of core registry functions.
generated_requirements.rationale: The system must remain functional across the full spectrum of military readiness states.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall maintain an audit log of all registry transactions, including submissions, modifications, deletions, searches, downloads, and access control changes, with timestamps and user identifiers.
generated_requirements.rationale: Audit logging is required for accountability, forensic analysis, and compliance with defense security regulations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support versioning of XML schemas and tags, preserving prior versions and enabling consumers to reference specific historical versions.
generated_requirements.rationale: Version control ensures backward compatibility and traceability of schema evolution over time.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall notify registered producers and consumers of changes to XML schemas or tags they have subscribed to, via configurable alerting mechanisms.
generated_requirements.rationale: Proactive notification ensures stakeholders are aware of relevant registry changes that may affect their applications.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support import and export of XML registry data in standard interchange formats to facilitate migration and interoperability with external registries.
generated_requirements.rationale: Import/export capabilities are needed for data sharing with allied systems and external defense registries.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce XML well-formedness and schema validity checks on all artifacts submitted to the registry before acceptance.
generated_requirements.rationale: Pre-acceptance validation prevents malformed or non-conformant data from polluting the registry.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a workflow mechanism for manager review and approval of producer submissions before they are published to the registry.
generated_requirements.rationale: An approval workflow ensures quality control and governance over registry content.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall respond to consumer search queries and return results within 3 seconds under normal operational load.
generated_requirements.rationale: Timely search responses are critical for operational efficiency, especially during crisis and wartime operations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support a minimum of 500 concurrent users across all roles (producers, consumers, managers) without degradation of response times beyond defined thresholds.
generated_requirements.rationale: Defense organizations involve large numbers of simultaneous users requiring consistent system performance.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall achieve an availability of at least 99.9% uptime during peacetime operations, measured on a monthly basis.
generated_requirements.rationale: High availability is essential for a defense information infrastructure that supports continuous operations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall remain operational and provide degraded-mode registry access (read-only browsing and download) when network connectivity to the central node is lost.
generated_requirements.rationale: Forward-deployed units must retain access to critical XML resources even under degraded communications conditions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall encrypt all data in transit using a minimum of TLS 1.2 or higher to protect XML registry data from interception.
generated_requirements.rationale: Encryption in transit is mandatory to protect sensitive defense information from eavesdropping on military networks.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall encrypt all sensitive data at rest within the RDBMS using approved cryptographic algorithms compliant with FIPS 140-2 or successor standards.
generated_requirements.rationale: Encryption at rest protects classified and sensitive registry data from unauthorized physical access.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall authenticate all users via multi-factor authentication (MFA) before granting access to any registry function.
generated_requirements.rationale: MFA is required to meet defense identity assurance requirements and prevent unauthorized access.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall automatically terminate idle user sessions after a configurable inactivity timeout not to exceed 15 minutes.
generated_requirements.rationale: Session timeout reduces the risk of unauthorized access through unattended authenticated sessions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide a web-based user interface that conforms to Section 508 accessibility standards and requires no more than 2 hours of training for a new consumer user to perform basic registry operations.
generated_requirements.rationale: Ease of use and accessibility are required to support a diverse military user base with varying technical backgrounds.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide context-sensitive online help and user documentation accessible from every page of the web-based interface.
generated_requirements.rationale: Integrated help reduces training burden and supports users in operational environments where formal training may be limited.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall recover from any single component failure and restore full service within 30 minutes without loss of committed registry transactions.
generated_requirements.rationale: Rapid recovery from failures is critical to maintaining continuous defense information operations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall perform automated daily backups of all registry data and configuration, with backups stored in a geographically separate location.
generated_requirements.rationale: Regular backups ensure data can be restored following catastrophic failure or data corruption events.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall have a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of no less than 2,000 hours for all critical registry services.
generated_requirements.rationale: A high MTBF ensures the system can sustain prolonged operations without service interruption.

generated_requirements.text: The system's source code and architecture shall adhere to modular design principles, with documented APIs and interfaces, to facilitate future maintenance and enhancement.
generated_requirements.rationale: Modular, well-documented code reduces the cost and risk of future modifications to the defense system.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall include automated regression test suites covering at least 80% of functional requirements, executable upon each software release.
generated_requirements.rationale: Automated testing ensures that updates and patches do not introduce regressions into critical registry functions.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall log all errors and exceptions with sufficient diagnostic detail to enable root-cause analysis without access to production data.
generated_requirements.rationale: Detailed error logging accelerates troubleshooting and reduces system downtime during maintenance.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be deployable on a minimum of two operating system platforms (e.g., Windows Server and a POSIX-compliant OS such as Linux) without modification to core application code.
generated_requirements.rationale: Cross-platform portability is required to support the heterogeneous hardware and OS environments found across defense installations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall be deployable in both fixed-installation data center environments and on ruggedized mobile computing platforms used by forward-deployed units.
generated_requirements.rationale: The system must operate across the full range of physical deployment environments encountered in military operations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall scale horizontally to accommodate a 300% increase in registry data volume and user load without requiring architectural redesign.
generated_requirements.rationale: Scalability ensures the system can grow with expanding defense data management needs without costly re-engineering.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall complete XML schema validation for documents up to 10 MB in size within 5 seconds under normal operational load.
generated_requirements.rationale: Validation performance must meet operational tempo requirements, particularly during time-sensitive crisis operations.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall detect and alert managers to unauthorized access attempts or repeated authentication failures within 60 seconds of occurrence.
generated_requirements.rationale: Rapid intrusion detection is critical to protecting sensitive defense registry data from active threats.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall synchronize replicated registry nodes and resolve conflicts using a defined conflict-resolution policy within 5 minutes of network connectivity being restored after a degraded-communications period.
generated_requirements.rationale: Timely and consistent replication reconciliation ensures data integrity after periods of disconnected operation.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall provide role-based dashboards for managers that display real-time registry health metrics, active user sessions, pending approvals, and replication status.
generated_requirements.rationale: Managers require situational awareness of registry operations to govern the system effectively.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall support the generation of standardized reports on registry usage, schema adoption rates, user activity, and audit log summaries, exportable in PDF and CSV formats.
generated_requirements.rationale: Reporting capabilities support management oversight, compliance verification, and strategic planning for the defense data enterprise.

generated_requirements.text: The system shall enforce a maximum registry entry size limit and reject submissions that exceed defined thresholds, returning a descriptive error message to the producer.
generated_requirements.rationale: Size enforcement protects system resources and prevents denial-of-service conditions caused by excessively large submissions.

ground_truth_requirements.text: Environmental Mode: This is the real-time operations that must react to varying degrees of readiness to full scale wartime operations such as crisis planning with the use of heterogeneous data types and sources, transfer capabilities, data management services. COE Compliance. XML Services shall be segmented. COE sponsors shall adhere to compliance level requirements described in the I&RTS. In the fixed (static) mode of operation (base or data processing megacenter), the data management services shall have the capability of being tuned by on-site personnel to adjust for varying workloads and sizes of associated databases. These workloads and databases are expected to change more frequently and to a greater extent than for processing associated with deployed units In a changing (dynamic) environment, such as with deployed units, the workload and database sizes may be more predetermined (given a more precise mission) and require access to fewer data management administrative capabilities than needed in a fixed environment. The XS shall have the ability to redefine or reset names of connect descriptors to database server instances. Connect descriptors are fully qualified object names and include address (protocol/host/port) and instance name. In a degraded communications environment, there is a need, for example, to be able to reset session time-out values if the data management services are being accessed by users affected by the communications degradation. At a minimum, the session time-out values shall be user definable and be able to be reset prior to initialization of a user session. The goal is to provide the option of dynamically changing session time values based on current communications performance identified by capabilities of the network management or DBMS. End User Mode: Portion of XS services shall be used by various classes of users: data consumers, data and database managers, network information infrastructure resource managers. Some of these uses of the data management services will entail unique requirements that shall be fulfilled within the capability of XS services. Maintenance Mode: This mode includes modification and/or addition of application data segments, user permission, privileges, and restructuring storage and memory areas. In addition, maintenance also shall pertain to shutdown, open not-mounted and online/off-line implementations, modifications, upgrade, or other related actions. The data management services shall support managing various types of data, database architectures and platforms that includes hardware and software at the specified sites. Training Mode. In support of training activities, the data management services shall provide for the same processing as would be encountered in a production environment. However, access to the database may be via a training application access to the DBMS rather than from the production mission application.

The Package is to be compressed and emailed or sent ftp to an addressee. Submit prescribed metadata related to information resource type, information resource association, status code, data types specified and other related information specified in Appendix A within a combination of forms. These forms are part of the Information Resource Submittal Package containing the following Information Resource artifacts: XML Tag Specification, XML Spec (i.e. DTD, DCD etc.), Sample of XML document of the tag to be submitted. ground_truth_requirements.text: COE Software will be qualified through formal validation tests of the SRS level requirements. The Qualification Methods applied to the software shall include test, demonstration, analysis, and inspection (T, D, A, I). COE Software will be qualified through formal validation tests of the SRS level requirements. The Qualification Methods applied to the software shall include test, demonstration, analysis, and inspection (T, D, A, I). TEST. A qualification method that is carried out by operation of the item/component/I/F (or some part of the computer S/W configuration item, etc.) and that relies on the collection and subsequent examination of data. DEMONSTRATIONS A qualification method that is carried out by operation of the item/component/I/F (or some part of the computer S/W configuration item, etc.), and that relies on observable functional operation not requiring the use of elaborate instrumentation or special test equipment. ANALYSIS A qualification method that is carried out by the processing of accumulated data. An example of accumulated data is the compilation of data obtained from other qualification methods. Examples of the processing of accumulated data are interpretations or extrapolations made from the data. INSPECTION A qualification method that is carried out by visual examination, physical manipulation, or measurement to verify that the requirements have been satisfied

ground_truth_requirements.text: Automatically: Indicates processing initiated during execution of other processes, but dependent on information and/or parameters to be generated or supplied to these other processes. The information / parameters may be data dependent, or application dependent, or dependent on a manual process/human intervention. It will include controls qualifying the processing involved. Business Rule: A narrative description of policies, procedures, or principles within an organization. Business rules can be divided in to four categories: definitions, facts, constraints, and derivations. Definitions are business rules that define entities and attributes. Facts are either links (relationships) between entities or associations between an entity and attributes Constraints are conditions about the data that must always be true. They are the integrity rules that protect the data in the eventual database. Derivations are business rules that materialize a new piece of information (often attribute values) from other pieces of information. For example, a mathematical derivation might specify that you can obtain a person's age by subtracting his or her birth date from the current date. Commit/Rollback: An individual transaction is processed (commit) or discarded (rollback) by the proponent maintainer of the data items involved. Discretionary Access Controls (DAC): A means of restricting access to objects based on the identity of subjects or groups to which they belong. The controls are discretionary in the sense that a subject with a certain access permission is capable of passing that permission on to any other subject. Dynamically Generated Processing: Indicates processing initiated during execution of other processes, but dependent upon information and/or parameters to be generated or supplied to these other processes. The information/parameters may be data dependent, or application dependent, or dependent on a manual process/human intervention. It will include controls qualifying the processing involved. Location Transparency: occurs when the physical location of data is transparent to the applications and users of the database system. For example, a view that joins table data from several databases provides location transparency because the user of the view does not need to know where the data originates from. Mandatory Access Control (MAC): Imediates access to an object based on the clearance level of the subject (user) and the sensitivity label of the object. (These controls are always enforced above any discretionary control implemented by users). Mirrored Databases: Replication and maintenance of a database on a transaction basis for the purpose of rapid error or failure recovery as supported by the resident COTS RDBMS own system utilities and operating system. Object: A passive entity that contains or receives information. Access to an object potentially implies access to the information it contains. Examples of objects are records, blocks, pages, segments, files, directories, directory trees, and programs, as well as bits, bytes, words, fields, processors, video displays, keyboards, clocks, printers, and network nodes. Proponent Scheme: Describes the sites at which databases are replicated and also who owns and has update authority with respect to the data at each site. It refers to proponency at the source and record level. Redundant Array of Inexpensive Disks (RAID): A RAID system appears as one very large, reliable disk to the CPU. The main reason for using RAID storage is its reliability. RAID has the same advantages as shadowing and striping at a lower cost. There are several types/levels of RAID implementations, including: RAID 0 (known as disk striping), RAID 1 (known as disk shadowing), RAID 3 (in which data is distributed in small increments across all data disks and adds a parity value to a separate disk for recovery if any disk fails, RAID 4 (in which data is distributed in large chunks across all data disks and also has a single parity disk. RAID 4 intended to overcome performance penalties of RAID 3 for small transfers. RAID 5 (in which parity over RAID 3 or RAID 4 implementations), and RAID 6 ( in which two parity disks in addition to data disks are used in an attempt to further improve performance). In a RAID 5 implementation, the data is stored as are check sums and other information about the contents of each disk in the array. If one disk is lost, the others can use the check sums and other stored information to recreate the lost data. Storage system vendors may provide additional enhancements to RAID level implementations to improve performance and reliability. Remote Data Access (RDA): is an ISO (9579) application layer interoperability standard (protocol and formats) to support access by an application to a (remote) DBMSs over an OSI network. The goal of RDA is to allow interoperability between applications (clients) and databases (servers) of different manufacturers so that an application is able to read and update data in remote databases via well defined standards. RDA defines a set of client and server standards and a mapping of SQL commands to these services. RDA also defines an interface to ISO (transaction processing) two phase commit TP services in the case where updates to multiple remote databases need to be coordinated. RDA does not yet define interoperability between server databases (i.e. it is not yet a standard for distributed database management). Replication Scheme: Information that precisely identifies DBs, or partitions of DBs, to be copied and/or distributed, replication schedules, and master/remote sites that are to receive the copies. Spatial DBMS: Geographic information system that organizes and maintains spatial data (i.e. data with graphical attributes) in terms of type, scale, location(s), extent, topology and geometry. Supports queries of spatial data where the selection criteria are defined by spatial attributes. SRI: A Standing Request For Information (SRI) is a capability in which CASS monitors for the occurrence of conditions established by an application program, and notifies the calling or establishing application program when the conditions are satisfied. An SRI may be one of three types: timer-based, data-based, or message-based. Subject: An active entity, generally in the form of a person, process, or device that causes information to flow among objects or changes the system state. Technically, a process/domain pair. Transaction Journalling: Individual messages or database transactions are stored in a journal file, which may be a linear log file or a circular file.

ground_truth_requirements.text: If the term is well recognized outside its container term, designate it as an element. Example: CTRY_CD, CTRY_NM, CTRY_ABBRD_NM, CTRY_OFF_NM, CTRY_SCP_NT_TX, and CTRY_PSTL_NM are all characteristics (ER-attributes) for the entity CTRY. In the relational world, they are columns within the table ctry, attributes of the entity ctry in er-modeling, and member attributes in object modeling. It would be expected that a submitter would identify them as attributes rather than elements. But if they were identified as attributes of the element ctry, then the additional baggage (other attributes) must be carried, or submitted as separate elements. We wish to limit the proliferation of tags, so we strongly urge folks to use XML_Attributes sparingly. If the term is well recognized outside its container term, designate it as an element.
